text: Robotic resection of gastric cardia mass. The patient is a seventy nine year old male with a history of early lung cancer. He was asymptomatic but was found to have an exophytic mass of the gastric cardia identified on a surveillance CT of the chest. EGD revealed a submucosal mass and FNA returned suspicious for GIST. A CT abdomen pelvis was then performed, showing a six centimeter exophytic submucosal mass at the gastric cardio along the lesser curve. He was treated with preoperative Gleevec for five months, but this was discontinued when no change in size was seen.

The preoperative CT scan shows a large exophytic mass near the gastroesophageal junction. A diagram illustrates the port placement for the robotic procedure, with three 8mm trocars positioned across the left upper abdomen and one 12mm trocar in the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament and dissects circumferentially around the hiatus. A Penrose drain is placed around the distal esophagus for retraction and identification of the GE junction. Monopolar scissors are used to dissect at the inferior border of the gastric mass, adjusting the margin to preserve normal gastric tissue.

The surgeon rotates the gastric mass laterally and continues dissection on its medial aspect. The final attachments are divided, and the mass is repositioned. The gastrotomy is evaluated to ensure it can be closed without tension and does not involve the gastroesophageal junction. The gastrotomy is closed in two layers: the first with absorbable barbed suture in a continuous fashion, and the second with braided permanent suture in an interrupted fashion.

After closing the gastrotomy, an EGD scope is inserted into the antrum and retroflexed to evaluate the closure, visible on the right side of the screen. The suture line shows no bleeding, and the GE junction is not narrowed. The stomach is desufflated, the scope is removed, and the specimen is extracted through the twelve millimeter port site, which is then closed with an ovicral suture.

The patient was started on a clear liquid diet on post-operative day one and discharged home on post-operative day two on a regular diet. The final pathology revealed a gastrogliomyoma rather than a just tumor.
